slides.title: JSX is Just Sugar 🍬
slides.content:
# `React.createElement` When you write JSX, a tool like Babel transforms it into `React.createElement` calls. This is why you need `import React from 'react'` even if you don't explicitly use React in your code (in React 17+, with the new JSX transform, you don't need to import React for JSX). For example: ```jsx

Hello

``` transpiles to: ```javascript React.createElement('h1', { className: 'title' }, 'Hello'); ``` `React.createElement` returns a plain JavaScript object called a **React element**. React then uses these objects to build the Virtual DOM.

slides.title: Compare JSX and createElement

quizData.question: What does JSX ultimately compile down to?
quizData.explanation: JSX is syntactic sugar for `React.createElement`, which returns plain objects (React elements).

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## New JSX Transform

In React 17+, the new JSX transform automatically imports special functions from the new JSX runtime, so you no longer need `import React from 'react'` in every file. Create React App uses this by default.
